# Prompt 04 — Breakdown PRs

## Purpose

Use this prompt to transform an approved technical plan into a clear pull request breakdown.

The goal is to split work into small, reviewable, independently validatable pull requests.

A good PR breakdown reduces regression risk, avoids oversized changes, and makes implementation easier to review, test, and rollback.

## When to use

Use this prompt when:

- a technical plan is already defined;
- the work is too large for one safe pull request;
- implementation needs to happen incrementally;
- refactoring needs to be separated from feature work;
- validation needs to be planned per PR;
- the team needs a clear execution sequence;
- Codex needs to implement one PR at a time.

Do not use this prompt to implement the PR directly.

## Recommended skill

Use this prompt with:

```txt
.agents/skills/pr-orchestrator/SKILL.md
```

Supporting skills:

```txt
.agents/skills/tech-lead/SKILL.md
.agents/skills/tester/SKILL.md
.agents/skills/build-and-validate/SKILL.md
```

Use `tech-lead` to verify technical sequencing.

Use `tester` to check whether each PR is independently testable.

Use `build-and-validate` to define realistic validation per PR.

## Required input

Before running this prompt, provide:

```md
# PR Breakdown Input

## Requirement

[Paste or reference the approved requirement]

## Functional specification

[Paste or reference the approved functional specification]

## Technical plan

[Paste or reference the approved technical plan]

## Current project constraints

[Known constraints, existing patterns, compatibility requirements, or delivery limits]

## Validation expectations

[Known commands, manual checks, CI checks, or required validation]

## Release constraints

[Deployment sequencing, feature flags, environment constraints, or rollback concerns]

## Out of scope

[What must not be included in this PR breakdown]

## Notes

[Any assumptions, known risks, related files, or previous decisions]
```

## Instructions for Codex

You are creating a pull request breakdown.

Follow these rules:

1. Start from the approved technical plan.
2. Preserve the agreed requirement and specification scope.
3. Split work into the smallest useful PRs.
4. Make each PR independently reviewable.
5. Make each PR independently validatable when possible.
6. Keep refactors separate from behavior changes when possible.
7. Keep dependency changes separate from implementation when possible.
8. Keep deployment changes separate from unrelated code changes.
9. List expected files per PR.
10. Define validation per PR.
11. Define regression risks per PR.
12. Identify dependencies between PRs.
13. Recommend the execution order.
14. Do not implement code.

## PR breakdown process

Follow this process:

1. Restate the implementation goal.
2. Identify work categories.
3. Identify dependency order.
4. Separate preparation work from behavior changes.
5. Separate implementation from validation when useful.
6. Separate risky changes into smaller PRs.
7. Define each PR title and objective.
8. Define included and excluded scope per PR.
9. List expected files per PR.
10. Define acceptance criteria per PR.
11. Define validation per PR.
12. Define regression risks per PR.
13. Give a final execution recommendation.

## Output format

Codex should respond using this structure:

```md
# Pull Request Breakdown

## Summary

Short explanation of the breakdown strategy.

## Source inputs

- Requirement:
- Functional specification:
- Technical plan:

## Breakdown strategy

Explain why the work is split this way.

## Execution order

1. PR 1 — [Title]
2. PR 2 — [Title]
3. PR 3 — [Title]

## PRs

### PR 1 — [Title]

#### Objective

One sentence describing the goal.

#### Scope

##### Included

- Item 1
- Item 2

##### Not included

- Item 1
- Item 2

#### Expected files

- `path/to/file` — expected change

#### Acceptance criteria

- Criterion 1
- Criterion 2

#### Validation

##### Automated checks

- `command`

##### Manual checks

- Step 1
- Step 2

#### Regression risks

- Risk 1
- Risk 2

#### Dependencies

- None
- Depends on PR X

#### Final status

Ready to implement | Needs clarification | Too broad | Blocked

### PR 2 — [Title]

#### Objective

One sentence describing the goal.

#### Scope

##### Included

- Item 1
- Item 2

##### Not included

- Item 1
- Item 2

#### Expected files

- `path/to/file` — expected change

#### Acceptance criteria

- Criterion 1
- Criterion 2

#### Validation

##### Automated checks

- `command`

##### Manual checks

- Step 1
- Step 2

#### Regression risks

- Risk 1
- Risk 2

#### Dependencies

- Depends on PR 1

#### Final status

Ready to implement | Needs clarification | Too broad | Blocked

## Cross-PR risks

| Risk   | Severity            | Affected PRs | Mitigation   |
| ------ | ------------------- | ------------ | ------------ |
| Risk 1 | High / Medium / Low | PR 1, PR 2   | Mitigation 1 |

## Out of scope for this breakdown

- Item 1
- Item 2

## Assumptions

- Assumption 1
- Assumption 2

## Open questions

- Question 1
- Question 2

## Final recommendation

Proceed with PR 1 | Needs clarification | Split further | Not recommended
```

## PR sizing rules

A PR is probably too large when it:

- changes many unrelated files;
- mixes frontend, backend, database, CI/CD, and documentation without a clear reason;
- includes refactor and feature behavior together;
- changes public contracts and consumers in the same large step;
- cannot be validated independently;
- cannot be reviewed in one focused session;
- has unclear rollback behavior;
- includes unrelated formatting changes.

A PR is probably well-sized when it:

- has one clear objective;
- changes a limited set of related files;
- can be reviewed independently;
- can be validated with clear checks;
- preserves the project in a working state;
- has explicit regression risks;
- can be reverted without undoing unrelated work.

## Recommended PR categories

Use these categories when useful.

### Documentation PR

Use for project docs, requirements, specs, technical plans, runbooks, or agent rules.

### Preparation PR

Use for small setup work needed before behavior changes.

Examples:

- create folder structure;
- add empty extension points;
- add non-invasive utilities;
- add missing tests around existing behavior.

### Refactor PR

Use only when the refactor is needed and independently safe.

Rules:

- preserve behavior;
- avoid adding new features;
- include regression validation;
- keep scope narrow.

### Feature PR

Use for implementing new user-visible or system behavior.

Rules:

- follow the approved spec;
- include acceptance criteria;
- include validation;
- list changed behavior.

### Test PR

Use for adding or improving tests when useful.

Rules:

- tests should validate behavior;
- avoid tests coupled only to implementation details;
- include known gaps.

### Validation PR

Use for build, CI, lint, typecheck, E2E, or quality gates.

Rules:

- avoid unrelated feature changes;
- document command changes;
- document CI impact.

### Deployment PR

Use for release, CI/CD, infrastructure, environment, or deployment-related changes.

Rules:

- isolate operational risk;
- include smoke tests;
- include rollback notes.

## Dependency rules

When sequencing PRs:

- documentation can come before implementation when it clarifies scope;
- tests can come before implementation when protecting existing behavior;
- refactors should come before features only when they reduce implementation risk;
- feature flags should come before risky feature rollout when needed;
- deployment changes should come after validation unless required earlier;
- cleanup should come after behavior is stable.

Do not create artificial dependencies between PRs.

## Validation rules

Each PR must define how it will be validated.

Validation may include:

- lint;
- format check;
- typecheck;
- unit tests;
- integration tests;
- E2E tests;
- build;
- manual checks;
- accessibility checks;
- smoke tests;
- deployment checks.

Use project-defined commands when available.

If commands are unknown, state that command discovery is required before implementation.

## Regression rules

Each PR must identify possible regressions.

Regression risks should mention:

- affected flows;
- affected files;
- affected contracts;
- affected environments;
- affected user roles;
- affected data;
- affected validation commands.

Do not use vague risks like:

```md
May break something.
```

Use specific risks like:

```md
Changing route middleware may regress unauthenticated redirects for protected dashboard pages.
```

## Quality criteria

The PR breakdown is acceptable when:

- it follows the approved technical plan;
- PRs are small and purposeful;
- each PR has a clear objective;
- each PR has explicit scope;
- each PR has expected files;
- each PR has validation steps;
- each PR has regression risks;
- dependencies are clear;
- out-of-scope items are explicit;
- the first PR is ready to implement;
- no PR is too broad to review safely.

## Common mistakes to avoid

- creating one large PR for everything;
- splitting PRs by arbitrary file type instead of reviewable value;
- mixing refactor with behavior change without reason;
- hiding risky changes inside cleanup;
- skipping validation per PR;
- leaving dependencies unclear;
- creating PRs that leave the project broken;
- adding deployment changes too early;
- creating too many tiny PRs with no useful review value;
- implementing code during the breakdown step.

## Example usage

```md
Use `prompts/04-breakdown-prs.md`.

# PR Breakdown Input

## Requirement

Authenticated users should be able to access the dashboard. Unauthenticated users should be redirected to login.

## Functional specification

Authenticated users can access `/dashboard`. Unauthenticated users are redirected to `/login`. Logged-in users should not access `/login`.

## Technical plan

Use existing session composable and route middleware. Do not add role-based permissions. Validate authenticated and unauthenticated navigation.

## Current project constraints

Use existing auth/session patterns. Keep dashboard layout unchanged.

## Validation expectations

Run lint, typecheck, build, and manual navigation checks.

## Release constraints

No deployment changes in this breakdown.

## Out of scope

Role-based permissions, password reset, user profile.

## Notes

Keep the first PR focused on route protection.
```

## Final instruction

Create the PR breakdown only.

Do not implement code, modify files, create commits, or continue to the next PR unless explicitly requested.
